title: Provisioning vs Configuration Management
description:
## Which Tool Category Fits the Task?

Two related but distinct categories of tooling exist:

- **Provisioning** tools (Terraform, CloudFormation) **create the infrastructure itself** — VMs, networks, load balancers, databases.
- **Configuration management** tools (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) **configure software on machines that already exist** — install packages, edit config files, manage services.

The task: *"Spin up three brand-new virtual machines, a private network, and a load balancer in the cloud from scratch."* Which category best fits, and why?

options.text: **Provisioning** (e.g. Terraform) — the task is creating new cloud infrastructure that does not yet exist.
**Configuration management** (e.g. Ansible) — the task is installing and configuring software on the machines.
Either one works identically; provisioning and configuration management are just two names for the same thing.
Neither — creating VMs and load balancers can only be done manually in the cloud console.

explanation: Creating brand-new VMs, networks, and load balancers is **provisioning** — bringing infrastructure into existence — which is the job of tools like Terraform and CloudFormation. **Configuration management** (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) operates on machines that *already exist*, installing packages and editing configs. They are complementary, not interchangeable: a common pipeline uses Terraform to provision the servers, then Ansible to configure the software on them. So the task described — building infrastructure from scratch — is squarely provisioning.

hints.title: Does the resource exist yet?
hints.body: If you're CREATING infrastructure that doesn't exist, that's provisioning. If you're configuring software ON existing machines, that's configuration management.
